rules_fired.because: High vapor pressure (≥5 kPa at 20°C) causes significant evaporation inside the tip. Without pre-wetting, the headspace is dry air; the solvent flash-evaporates into it, creating a vapour cushion that compresses and releases unpredictably, drifting dispensed volume by 5–15%. Three aspirate-expel cycles saturate the headspace before the actual dispensing step. Fast dispense minimises dwell-time in the tip.
rules_fired.cite: Eppendorf Application Note: Pipetting Volatile Liquids, 2018. NIST WebBook CAS 64-17-5 (vapor pressure 5.95 kPa at 20°C).

rules_fired.because: Light-sensitive reagents degrade under UV or visible light by different mechanisms depending on the compound class. Fluorophores (DAPI, ethidium bromide) undergo irreversible photobleaching — the chromophore is destroyed; 30 seconds of UV transilluminator exposure causes measurable DAPI signal loss. Oxidizers (H₂O₂, NaOCl) photodecompose, losing titer. Peroxide-forming solvents (diethyl ether) form hydroperoxides faster in light. Light-activated monomers (acrylamide) can begin polymerising spontaneously. Amber containers or foil-wrap block the relevant wavelengths for all of these.
rules_fired.cite: Thermo Fisher Scientific: DAPI Technical Data Sheet (D1306). Lakowicz, Principles of Fluorescence Spectroscopy, 3rd ed., Ch. 1. Sigma-Aldrich H₂O₂ Product Information (216763).

rules_fired.because: Ethers (diethyl ether, THF, dioxane) form shock-sensitive hydroperoxides on exposure to air and light via autoxidation. Peroxide concentration increases with time and is highest in the last few millilitres as the solvent evaporates. Opening a bottle without a peroxide test when the contents are old or unknown is a documented explosion hazard. Max 3-month working life after opening; test with commercial peroxide strips before each use; discard at 25 ppm peroxide or above.
rules_fired.cite: Jackson HL et al. (1998) Chem Health Safety 5(3): 16–18 (ether peroxide hazards). Prudent Practices in the Laboratory, NRC 2011, Ch. 6.
